======================================================================
PAGE: Tû
CATEGORIES: Characters in The Book of Lost Tales, Characters in The History of Middle-earth
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Tû
======================================================================

!
This content is considered pre-canon.
While the subject of this article is based on official information, it was replaced, emended, or abandoned in later stages of the
legendarium
.
!
Tû
is a character of earlier versions of
J.R.R. Tolkien
's legendarium.
History
Tû appeared in a fragmentary version of Tolkien's working notes for the
Coming of Men
, given in
The Book of Lost Tales Part Two
as "
Gilfanon's Tale
". Tolkien apparently decided not to incorporate the tale into
his mythos
, as it did not survive to become part of the chapter of
The Silmarillion
entitled "
Of the Coming of Men into the West
".
Tû is described as a "wizard" or "fay" who ruled the
Dark Elves
of the further east of
Middle-earth
, around
Cuivienen
, just before the making of the Sun. One of his subjects,
Nuin
"Father of Speech", came to a hidden valley of great loveliness and found there sleeping figures young but not children, boys and girls: the Fathers of
Men
. He told this to Tu, who fell into great fear of the One, and told Nuin that these were the Fathers of
Men
, who were to be undisturbed. Notes then tell that Nuin awoke two of the Sleepers,
Ermon
and
Elmir
, and taught them speech; that Tû and Nuin shepherded the Sleepers when they awoke with the
Sun
, and that
Men
split into factions in the years afterward. Tû grew to shun day and took to dwelling underground. A demon named
Fankil
, son of
Melkor
, arose and set
Men
at each other's throats, and especially against the
Elves
. The folk of Ermon stood with the Elves, but Tû came forth and destroyed Fankil, and was buried under falling hills.
What race or manner of being Tû was is unknown; he was described a great magician and a being of power, benevolent but testy, and afterwards came to have great hatred of
Men
for the deeds they did to his Elves.

======================================================================
PAGE: Ufedhin
CATEGORIES: Characters in The Book of Lost Tales, Elves, Elves of Beleriand, Elves of Doriath, Grey Elves
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Ufedhin
======================================================================

!
This content is considered pre-canon.
While the subject of this article is based on official information, it was replaced, emended, or abandoned in later stages of the
legendarium
.
!
Ufedhin
was an
Elf
of the
First Age
in earlier versions of the
legendarium
,  notable for his treachery against
Thingol
,
King of Doriath
.
History
Ufedhin was significantly one of the corrupt Elves who helped treacherous
Dwarves
from
Belegost
and
Nogrod
past the
Girdle of Melian
, and also helping to incite the Dwarves already inside the
Thousand Caves
to war in order to seize the cursed treasure of
Mîm
. He appears in both "
The Nauglafring
" and the later
Quenta Noldorinwa
, but then mysterious vanishes from other accounts of the sack of
Doriath
.
The novel
Beren and Lúthien
uses material from the later versions of the Quenta Noldorinwa which explains that "by the treachery of certain Elves on whom the lust of the accursed treasure had fallen they [dwarves] passed through into Doriath in secret."
[
citation needed
]
It appears
Christopher Tolkien
has taken a new route to explain how the Dwarves bypassed the veil (a thorn in
J.R.R. Tolkien
struggled with trying to explain and had several theories, including the 'inside help from Ufedhin, but never settled on one.)
The problem of the entry of the Dwarvish army into Doriath, defended by the Girdle of Melian, is still solved by the device - the too simple device, see II. 250 - of 'some treacherous Gnomes' (in the Tale there was only one traitor); the slaying of Thingol while hunting remains, and as in the Tale Melian, inviolable, left the Thousand Caves seeking Beren and Luthien. Though it is not so stated, it seems likely that in this version it was Melian who brought the news and the warn- ing to Beren (this is the story in the Quenta, pg. 160).
References

======================================================================
PAGE: Uin
CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Uin
======================================================================

!
The canonicity of this subject is uncertain.
The precise canonical status of this article's subject is unclear.
To find out about what is considered "canon" see
LOTR:Canon
.
!
Uin
Biographical information
Titles
Primeval whale
Great Whale
Gulma's
great whale
Birth
Elder Days
Death
On or after the
Faring Forth
Realms
Belegaer
Weapon
Might of the
Valar
Physical description
Race
Right Whales
Gender
Male
"
There are no other references to the dragging of Tol Eressëa across the Ocean by Uin the great whale, to the Isle of Íverin, or to the Battle of Rôs;
"
—
Christopher Tolkien
in
The Book of Lost Tales Part Two
Uin
, known as the
Great Whale
and
Gulma
's great whale
, was the
primeval
whale
of
Arda
that served the
Vala
Ulmo
.
Biography
During the
Elder Days
, when Ulmo gathered "a host of the greatest fish" to
Tol Eressëa
, Uin, "the mightiest and most ancient of whales", came among them alongside narwhals
and sea lions
. Ulmo directed them in using their strength to pull the island across
Belegaer
to the shores of
Hísilómë
in
Beleriand
. Once the
Teleri
were on the island, Uin and the rest of the host took them to the
Bay of Faëry
in
Valinor
before pulling the island back to Hísilómë.
On the trip back to Hísilómë,
Ossë
noticed Uin pulling the island and was jealous that his island was being used without his permission. Yet when he tried to pursue Uin, he was left far behind because Uin and the rest of the host were granted "the might of the
Valar
" by Ulmo. Thus, they reached Hísilómë again where the
Noldoli
got in the island. The second ferrying went as well as the first, save only for a minor annoyance from Ossë.
After the Noldoli were safely in Aman alongside the Teleri, Uin and the other great fish pulled the island for a final trip for the
Solosimpi
. However, "Ossë cast storms and shadows" to hinder a third trip, causing Ulmo to direct Uin through "devious ways", resulting in the great fish arrive late to Hísilómë. On the return trip back to Aman, Uin and the great fish were waylaid by Ossë and Uinen just as "the mists of the
Shadowy Seas
" were in sight. Ossë grabbed the island with his great hand, causing Uin, with all his great strength, to only be able to "scarcely drag it onward" since none of the Valar could beat Ossë with strength in water. With Uinen's aid, the island was anchored to the seafloor by making giant ropes out of the centuries old "leather-weeds and polyps" that grew "to unimagined girth" throughout the pillars of Ossë and Uinen's "deep-sea house".
Even with Ulmo aiding Uin and the great fish "with all his godlike power", Ossë gathered many rocks and boulders beneath the island and invited "every kind of deep sea creature" to build homes throughout "the base of the island". With the struggle intensifying and Ulmo vainly trumpeting, Uin stirred the seas into wrath with "his unmeasurable tail lash".
Eventually, Uin and the other great fish stopped pulling the island after Ulmo gave up and went back to
Váli-màr
.
A long time after, before the
Faring Forth
, the
Elves
convince Ulmo to allow Uin to aid them. As such, when Tol Eressëa is uprooted, Uin pulled it towards the
Great Lands
, almost "to the promontory of
Rôs
". Upon doing this, however, enraged Ossë, causing him to try to pull the island back. As a consequence, however, the island broke in two, with the western half becoming the
Isle of Íverin
, known today as Ireland
.
Etymology
Uin
is a
Gnomish
common noun meaning "whale", originating from the primeval whale, Uin himself. Originally, the word simply meant "wave", but this is only preserved in poetry.
A similar Gnomish word for "whale" was
uimoth
("sheep of the waves"), containing the word
moth
,
meaning "sheep or 1000" and indicating its original meaning of "flock" (e.g.
mothweg
means "shepherd").
Whether
uin
remained a
Sindarin
word in the later and final stages of
J.R.R. Tolkien
's
legendarium
is untold.
Uin
is also a
Qenya
word meaning "a fish, the primeval whale", derived from the root
IWI
("fish").
Uin
appears in the Qenya Lexicon together with "Queen of the Mermaids", the definition given for
Ui Oarista
, another name for
Uinen
. How that name relates to
Uin
is untold.
According to
Christina Scull
and
Wayne G. Hammond
,
Uin
means "I swim" in
Finnish
,
in which
valas
is the term to refer to whales. The similarity of etymology between the names of Uin and Uinen somewhat resembles the case of
Cetus
(Kētŏs) and
Ceto
(Keto) in the
Greek Mythology
; whale-related mythological creatures serving the prominent oceanic deities, and sea goddesses (both loved and feared by people), sharing the origins of the etymologies with the creatures.
In other works
In
Roverandom
, after Roverandom's adventure on the
Moon
and the seagull, Mew, took him back to the cove of the sand-sorcerer, he found Psamathos Psamathides having a discussion with Uin, who was very large and "the oldest of the
Right Whales
" (Despite this statement, however,
Christina Scull
and
Wayne G. Hammond
made the claim that Uin "is not quite the mightiest and eldest of his kind" in
Roverandom
unlike how in
The Book of Lost Tales Part One
, he is entitled as the primeval individual.
). The dog Rover described Uin as looking "like a mountain" and having "his great head in a deep pool". Uin allowed Rover to ride in his mouth on the journey to see the wizard Artaxerxes at the bottom of the
Deep Blue Sea
. According to Psamathos Psamathides, Uin was prone to coughs from scratches and bites. When Rover tried to back out, Mew and Psamathos Psamathides pushed him into Uin's mouth.
Unlike other whales, Uin was considerate enough that he did not swim with his mouth constantly open to catch food. Uin was said to be very exceptional among whales. He was capable of resting underwater for long periods of time. Uin was also capable of swimming across the world with ease. He also worked in some capacity for Psamathos Psamathides, for after Rover told him that Artaxerxes did not change him back, he reported to Psamathos Psamathides's cove. Uin had some ability of foresight, since after his tail knocked a sea-tree over, he predicted that Artaxerxes was going to be a failure at his current job.
Uin was kind enough to be willing to give the two Rovers multiple rides to many places in the
Outer Lands
and seas. During one of these rides, Uin took them to "the eastern edge of the world" where Uin let loose a burst of water that sailed "so high that a lot of it was thrown right off the world and over the edge". During another ride, Uin travelled as far as he dared to the other side of the world where they had many adventures in the
Uncharted Waters
. One of which involved Uin taking the Rovers pass the
Shadowy Seas
to the great
Bay of Fairyland
beyond the
Magic Isles
. Due to the light of
Faery
, Roverandom became one of the few living beings to have had a quick "glimpse of the
city
of the
Elves
on the
green hill
" that lay before the
Mountains of Elvenhome
"in the last
West
".
After Uin took a sudden dive, he became worried that he might "catch it" if it was ever discovered that he took the two Rovers to the
Bay of Fairyland
.
After the exile of Mr. and Mrs. Artaxerxes regarding the failure of the PAM to deal with the ancient Sea-serpent, Uin took the two and Roverandom on his back and swam towards the surface.
Uin took them to a shore quite distant from the cove of the sand-sorcerer, where he waited with Mrs. Artaxerxes while Mr. Artaxerxes (with Rover in his pocket) went to a sea-side town to make a few mundane purchases. When they returned, Mr. Artaxerxes smoked a pipe while he leaned against Uin.
Later on, when the wizard forgot that he did not destroy all of his belongings, Uin began winking. After Mr. Artaxerxes reversed Rover's curse, Rover thought that everything seemed smaller and funnier, including Uin. When Rover bid them all farewell, Uin winked at him. It was later suggested that Rover never found out what happened to Uin after he departed the beach.
Inspiration
The "host of the greatest fish" may represent a host of whales as
J.R.R. Tolkien
described in his 1957 letter to Michael George Tolkien regarding his translation of
The Book of Jonah
that disambiguation between "whale" and "fish" wasn't important to him during the translation.
Tolkien may have made Uin a Right Whale because the Genus of that species,
Eubalaena
, means "true  whale" or "good whale" and so on. Right whales were also historically one of the closest of all whales in terms of relating to human histories. Understandably for New Zealand cultures, right whales, being regarded as "(one of) the most important or symbolic whale to New Zealand" (hence given the Maori name "Tohorā" to represent whales
), can be seen as an important role in the creation myth of the nation, commonly known as the legend of "Whale Rider", or as a guardian deity called Taniwha.
Right whales' curious and playful natures sometimes allow humans and kayaks to ride on their back as well, possibly influencing Rover being able to ride Uin in
Roverandom
.
In adaptations
Middle-earth Role Playing
In the
Middle-earth Role Playing
,
Uin
is said to be a
merfolk
.
Aamumeren Isa
, a Maia, takes a form of a humpback whale with twice the size of normal adult humpback whales, is also a merfolk. Especially cetaceans are direct realms of Ulmo, and dolphins are called
Ulmodil
("friends of Ulmo"). Uin's etymology can be seen on several terms such as
Uini
meaning "whales", and Hûb-in-Uinin, the "Bay of Whales".

I Vene Kemen
map of
Arda

Gregthoth
.

Uin
as a
bowhead whale
from the
Middle-earth Roleplaying Wiki
Trivia
A Right Whale was named after
Gandalf
in 2020,
while a
humpback whale
(the species Alan Lee mistakenly depicted Uin in his illustration) was named after
Frodo Baggins
.

Turtle-fish
Fastitocalon

at
Wikipedia

CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth, Maiar, The Silmarillion characters
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Uinen
======================================================================

Uinen
Biographical information
Other names
Ónen, Solórë
Titles
Lady of the Sea, Spirit of the Sea, The Tempest Calmer, Calmer of the Sea
Birth
Before the creation of
Arda
Death
Immortal
Spouse
Ossë
Physical description
Race
Ainur
Culture
Maiar
(Maia of Ulmo)
Uinen
(
Sindarin
;
IPA
:
[ˈuɪnen]
) was a
Maia
spirit known as
The Lady of the Sea
and the wife of the Maia
Ossë
. She was a matron to all mariners. Uinen was third greatest of all the Maiar.
Biography
Before the Sun and the Moon
Uinen loved all creatures that lived in the saltwater streams of the
world
and the weeds that grew in them. Her love calmed the violent tidal waves and storms created by
Ossë
her husband by keeping him in line and not going too far. Her hair spread throughout waters and lies on their surfaces. During the early days
Arda
, Ossë rebelled against his Lord
Ulmo
(
Lord of All Waters
) and served
Melkor
instead, being carried away by the false promises that Melkor gave to him. For a while, there rose great tumults in the sea that wrought great ruins to the lands. Because of this, and because of the prayers of
Aulë
who created those lands, Uinen helped bring Ossë back to the allegiance of their lord Ulmo by restraining her husband and bringing him before Ulmo. Following that, Ossë was pardoned and gave his allegiance back to the
Valar
.
To the
Maiar
Uinen and Ossë, Ulmo gave them the task of the governing of the waves and the movements of the Inner Seas. Ever afterwards, Uinen was the one who restrained Ossë's bouts of violence in the seas and on the coasts.
Eventually, when the
Teleri
remained on the coast of the western seas, awaiting Ulmo to take them to
Aman
, Uinen and Ossë came among the Teleri and befriended them.
After the
Kinslaying at Alqualondë
, where
Fëanor
and his host of exiled Ñoldor attacked the Teleri and stole their ships, Uinen wept for the
Telerin
mariners who had been wickedly slain and tossed into the sea. The sea also rose in great wrath against the
Ñoldorin
slayers, so that many of the stolen Telerin ships were wrecked and the Ñoldor in them were drowned.
When the Hiding of
Valinor
took place, Ossë raised the enchanted Isles. Then next, Uinen enchanted those who ever managed to reach the Isles by weaving them in her hair before
Irmo
(
Master of Dreams & Desires
) put them to age-long sleep.
Second Age
In the
Second Age
, the mariners of
Númenór
would cry to Uinen for help, because she was the only one capable of calming Ossë who loved to create tumults in the seas. And the
Númenóreans
lived under her protection while their respect for the Valar endured. Therefore, the Númenóreans held the Maia Uinen in reverence as equal to the Valar.
Other versions of the legendarium
In
Númenórean
legend, it was told that Uinen had the Isle of
Tol Uinen
placed in the
Bay of Rómenna
as a gift to the people of Númenór.
In
J.R.R. Tolkien
's initial story of the
Sun
and the
Moon
, it was Uinen who wove the sails for the ship of the Moon. Uinen had made them as white mists and foam, and some were spread like "glittering scales of silver fish" while others were threaded with tiny stars.

======================================================================
PAGE: Ulbandi
CATEGORIES: Characters in The Book of Lost Tales
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Ulbandi
======================================================================

!
This content is considered pre-canon.
While the subject of this article is based on official information, it was replaced, emended, or abandoned in later stages of the
legendarium
.
!
Ulbandi
Biographical information
Other names
Fluithuin
Spouse
Melko
Physical description
Race
Ogres
Gender
Female
Ulbandi
, or
Fluithuin
, was an
ogress
who was the spouse of
Melko
and the mother of
Gothmog
, one of the
Valarindi
in earlier versions of the mythology.
Etymology
Ulbandi
was a
Quenya
name of an unknown meaning, though it contains the element
ulban(d)
("monster, giant").
Fluithuin
was a
Gnomish
name of an unknown meaning.
Development
Tolkien later discarded the idea that the Valar had children, and thus the character of Ulbandi was abandoned.
References

PAGE: Ulbar
CATEGORIES: Characters in Unfinished Tales, Númenóreans
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Ulbar
======================================================================

Ulbar
Biographical information
Birth
Early
Second Age
?
Death
Mid Second Age ?
Spouse
Unnamed wife
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Númenóreans
Ulbar
was a
Man of Númenór
.
Biography
Ulbar was a shepherd of
Emerië
turned mariner and father of
Îbal
. He originally tended sheep on a farm called
Hyarastorni
in Emerië that was owned by lord
Hallatan
. He married and woman and had one son, Îbal. When he heard of the formation of the
Guild of Venturers
by
Aldarion
, he joined it and became a crewman on the
Hirilondë
and sailed with him to and from
Middle-earth
and other lands as well. He was gone for many years.
When Ulbar returned to
Númenor
, he rode with Aldarion and
Henderch
to Emerië when Aldarion went to visit his
wife
. Ulbar did not stay and instead by his lord's leave went home to his house in Hyarastorni where his family and friends were awaiting his return. During a celebration of Ulbar's return, Aldarion came to the house desiring rest during which time he spoke to Ulbar's son Îbal about seafaring and gave a red jewel on a band of gold to his wife, as a reward for Ulbar's six years of service to him.
Ulbar greatly respected Aldarion calling him the "The Great Captain".

Uldor
Biographical information
Titles
The Accursed
Birth
YS 125
East of the
Blue Mountains
Death
YS 472
(aged 47)
Nírnaeth Arnoediad
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Easterlings
of
Morgoth
Uldor
, also known as
Uldor the Accursed
,
was an
Easterling
Man
, and the youngest son of
Ulfang the Black
.
Biography
Uldor was an Easterling who entered into the service of the
Sons of Fëanor
.
Serving with
Caranthir
since
FA 457
, Uldor and his brothers
Ulfast
and
Ulwarth
helped Caranthir prepare for war with
Morgoth
; but like the rest of his family, they were already secretly allied with him.
They agreed to betray
Maedhros
and his forces to Morgoth in the
Nírnaeth Arnoediad
by sending a series of false warnings of an attack from
Angband
from another point. This delayed the march of the Eastern Army under Maedhros from joining the rest of the
coalition
in time for the main battle.
He was the leader of the treacherous Easterlings after his father's death, and attacked the Eastern Army from their rear with a hidden force of Easterlings. He was slain by
Maglor
, but his actions helped Morgoth win the battle.
Genealogy
Ulfang
Uldor
Ulfast
Ulwarth

Ulfang
Biographical information
Titles
Chieftain of the Easterlings
Birth
FA 400
East of the
Blue Mountains
Death
FA 470
(aged 70)
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Easterlings
Ulfang
, also known as
Ulfang the Black
, was an
Easterling
chieftain of the
First Age
who followed
Caranthir
. His sons fought in the
Nírnaeth Arnoediad
but betrayed the
Fëanorians
and thus enabled the victory of
Morgoth
.
Biography
Ulfang was among the "Swarthy Men" who had come out of the East into
Beleriand
. He swore allegiance to Caranthir but was secretly in the service of Morgoth. In the battle his men turned on the
Sons of Fëanor
. Their force, which was being attacked on three sides, was dissipated, allowing Morgoth to concentrate his forces on
Fingon
and
Turgon
. Ulfang's treacherous progeny, however, paid for their betrayal with their lives, for
Uldor
,
Ulfast
, and
Ulwarth
all perished in the battle.
Genealogy
Ulfang
Uldor
Ulfast
Ulwarth

Ulfast
Biographical information
Birth
FA 428
East of the
Blue Mountains
Death
FA 472
(aged 44)
Nírnaeth Arnoediad
Weapon
Sword,
Bow
?
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Easterlings
of
Morgoth
Ulfast
was an
Easterling
of the
First Age
.
Biography
Ulfast was one of the three sons of
Ulfang
. He followed his father in the
Nírnaeth Arnoediad
where they betrayed the host of
Caranthir
. He was killed by the sons of
Bór
for he and his father's treachery against Caranthir.

Ulfang
Uldor
Ulfast
Ulwarth

======================================================================
PAGE: Ulmo
CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth, The Silmarillion characters, Valar
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Ulmo
======================================================================

Ulmo
Biographical information
Other names
Ullubôz, Ulubôz
Titles
King of the Sea,
Lord of Waters
Birth
Before the creation of
Arda
Death
Immortal
Realms
Valinor
(
Ulmonan
)
Spouse
None
Physical description
Race
Ainur
Height
Fairly tall
Hair
Gray
Eyes
Light gray
Culture
Valar
(
Aratar
)
Ulmo
(
Quenya
: "Pourer" or "Rainer"), also known as
Ulubôz
or
Ullubôz
, was an
Ainu
, one of the
Aratar
, and the
Vala
responsible for the control over the oceans of
Arda
. A lover of water, Ulmo was one of the Arda's chief architects and was always in a close friendship with
Manwë
. He always distrusted
Melkor
, and the
Dark Lord
feared the
Sea
almost as much as he feared
Varda
because the sea cannot be tamed. Ulmo had no dwelling in
Valinor
or any permanent dwelling on land, as he preferred the deeps of the seas and the
rivers
. His palace, on the bottom of
Vaiya
, was called
Ulmonan
.
Biography
Ulmo seldom came to the Councils of
Máhanaxar
, and only when in great need. He preferred to stay in Arda, not by walking on the land, as his form would fill
man
or
Elf
with great dread. All waters were under his government: bays, rivers and even the waters under the earth. Where the power of Morgoth was strong, however, Ulmo's own power would be sapped from waters near to Morgoth's corruption. It is through the rivers that he kept in touch with Arda, and thus knew more of the goings on with the
Children of Ilúvatar
than even Manwë, for in that way he lived in the very veins of the world.
Ulmo, appearing to Tuor, as imagined by
Roger Garland
His visible form caused fear in mortals and even the Elves. In the
Fall of Gondolin
he was described as having a tall crown like silver, and his hair was like shimmering sea foam. He wore a grey cloak which appeared as a mist veiling a coat of mail which glittered like fish scales flickering green.
He also invented a musical instrument called the
Ulumúri
, which he held when he hailed
Tuor
to lead him to Gondolin.
Ulmo always loved the
Eldar
and the
Edain
, and helped them even when the Valar forsook Middle-earth, a role to which he had been "appointed ere the making of the World".
He opposed
Oromë
's plan to bring the Elves to Aman, but helped those that were willing to reach it. He uprooted a large island which was later known as
Tol Eressëa
and used it to ferry them across the
Belegaer
to the
Blessed Realm
. Afterwards, he anchored it in the
Bay of Eldamar
, which he did because he knew the minds of the
Teleri
.
Ulmo was the Vala most responsible for the fall of
Morgoth
, by urging
Turgon
to build
Gondolin
and
Finrod
to build
Nargothrond
(
FA 50
). He appeared before
Tuor
and urged him to go to Gondolin as a messenger to Turgon. When he got there, he eventually married Turgon's daughter
Idril
and fathered
Eärendil
. Ulmo saved Eärendil's wife
Elwing
from the sack of the
Havens of Sirion
, allowing her to take a
Silmaril
to her husband, which allowed him to gain admittance to Valinor and plead for aid. Ulmo also defended them in the council from the potential wrath of
Mandos
.
Maiar of Ulmo
Ossë
, the vassal of Ulmo who guarded the waters around Middle-earth.
Uinen
, the wife of Ossë whose love for all sea creatures calmed the violent tidal waves and storms.
Salmar
, the music performer of Ulmo who created the Ulumuri.
Uin
, the "primeval whale" served under Ulmo. It is unknown whether this whale was actually an
Ainur
to have shape like other races of possible Ainur such as the
Great Eagles
.
Through these Maiar, Ulmo learned much of the Elves.
Etymology
Ulmo
means "pourer" or "rainer" in
Quenya
. In
Gnomish
, he was called
Gulma
.
Trivia
He was the only male Vala never to have married.
Ulmo shares similarities with Poseidon, the god of the sea in Classical Graeco-Roman mythology.
Usually he uses the form of an enormous wave, and he's rarely seen in his human form.
Gallery

======================================================================
PAGE: Ulrad
CATEGORIES: Characters in The Children of Húrin, The Silmarillion characters
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Ulrad
======================================================================

"... so small a thing. Such a little thing!"
This article is a
stub
; please
expand it
if you can.
"Can you see anything?" "Nothing. There's nothing."
The descriptive majority of this article's text is
unsourced
, and should be supported with
references
.
Ulrad
was one of the
Men
in the
band of outlaws
that was led by
Túrin Turambar
. He was a friend of the group's former leader,
Forweg
, whom Túrin had killed.
Mîm
the Petty-dwarf held Ulrad partly responsible for his son
Khîm
's death because Ulrad was one of the men that bound him.
Ulrad's later fate is untold.

Ulwarth

Ulwarth
was an
Easterling
Man
and the son of
Ulfang
.
Biography
Like his father, Ulwarth entered
Beleriand
and pledged his service to the
Sons of Fëanor
. They served under
Caranthir
, but their only true loyalty was to
Morgoth
, and their purpose was to betray him at the right time.
During a critical time during the
Nírnaeth Arnoediad
, he turned on Caranthir attacking his forces from the rear. When
Bór
and the other faithful Easterlings saw what they were doing, Bór and his men slew him and his father and brothers
Ulfast
and
Uldor
.

!
This content is considered pre-canon.
While the subject of this article is based on official information, it was replaced, emended, or abandoned in later stages of the
legendarium
.
!
Umuiyan
Biographical information
Other names
Gumniow
Titles
Clumsy-foot
Doorcat
Tevildo's doorkeeper
Birth
Elder Days
Death
First Age
Realms
Tiberth's hall
Physical description
Race
Cats
Gender
Male
Hair
Grey
Eyes
Yellow
Culture
Angamandi
(Cat-servants of
Tevildo
)
Umuiyan
, also known as
Gumniow
,
was the doorcat of
Tevildo's
stronghold in "
The Tale of Tinúviel
" in
The Book of Lost Tales
.
Biography
Umuiyan was a doorcat stationed at Tevildo's sunning ground, on the lowest terrace beneath the hill-top cliff that the stronghold stood on.
Some time after
Beren
was made a scullion by
Tevildo
,
Tinúviel
ventured to Tevildo's halls. Upon her approach, Umuiyan opened his yellow eyes and stretched on the rock before accusing her of trespassing on Tevildo's sunning ground. After Tinúviel asked to be brought to Tevildo himself, Umuiyan refused by lashing his tail; though he relented after Tinúviel called him a lord, loudly purring when his massive ugly head was stroked.
After tossing Tinúviel onto his back by seizing her garments by the shoulder, Umuiyan jumped from the first terrace to the second. After noting that he had a great desire to sleep and could not bear Tinúviel on his back anymore, he stretched, yawning, and led Tinúviel to meet the Prince of Cats at some baking stones further along that terrace. Umuiyan roused a sleeping Tevildo, reporting to him of Tinúviel's arrival and refusal to leave.
After bring intrigued by what Tinúviel had to say, Tevildo charged Umuiyan with bearing her to the kitchen of the stronghold. After bearing Tinúviel up two terraces, he stumbled when he landed on the third, causing Tevildo to suggest to him that he leaves his service due to getting swiftly older. To this, Umuiyan denied the accusation that he was getting old, claiming that a mist was hovering in front of him which was causing his head to be heavy. After staggering, Tinúviel feel off his back and Umuiyan went to sleep. This caused Tevildo to get angry at having to carry Tinúviel the rest of the way. As a consequence, Tevildo decided that the aging doorkeeper was too weak in his feet to be useful and, upon summoning his cat-servants, Tevildo ordered some of them to go to where Umuiyan slept and bind him before throwing him from the sheer cliff on the northern side of the hill-top.
Etymology
Umuiyan
may be a
Qenya
word, though it is not glossed.
Gumniow
may be a
Gnomish
cognate, though it is not glossed.
Other versions
Umuiyan and Tevildo would be excluded as characters from later versions of the
legendarium
.
References

======================================================================
PAGE: Ungoliant
CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth, The Silmarillion characters
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Ungoliant
======================================================================

Ungoliant
Biographical information
Other names
Del-du-thling
,
Ungweliantë
Titles
Gloomweaver,
Great Spider Who Enmeshes
Birth
Unknown, possibly after the
Ainulindalë
Death
Unknown,
First Age
;
suspected to have died by self-cannibalism
Realms
Utumno
,
Avathar
,
Ered Gorgoroth
, and "the forgotten South of the
world
"
Spouse
"other evil creatures of spider-form"
Weapon
Unlight
Physical description
Race
Spirit
in a
spider
-form
Gender
Female
Eyes
Black
Ungoliant
(
Sindarin
;
IPA
:
[uŋˈɡoljant]
), also known as
Gloomweaver
,
the
Great Spider Who Enmeshes
,
Del-du-thling
,
and
Ungoliantë
,
was a
spirit
who took on the form of a monstrous
Spider
. She was initially an ally of
Melkor
in
Aman
, and for a short time in
Middle-earth
as well. She was a distant mother of
Shelob
, and the oldest giant spider of
Arda
. She was "the primeval devourer of light"
and the destroyer of the
Two Trees of Valinor
.
Appearance
The origins of Ungoliant are unspecified, except that she had been a spirit, entering Arda and taking the form of a giant spider. As a spirit being, she would only be perceptible to those that could perceive spirits and the spirit world. It is implied that her spirit form was one of pure darkness, reflected in her physical form. Since the Valar, Maiar, and other spirits in the physical world freely took on forms that reflected their nature, Ungoliant's terrifying and monstrous form did the same. While her form was described as being that of a giant spider, and she is credited as the "first" giant spider, the narrative indicates that, much like her descendant Shelob, she had many features that distinguished her from the common form of a spider.
[
citation needed
]
Biography
Early years
Ungoliant and the Two Trees, by
Ted Nasmith
The true origins of Ungoliant are shrouded in mystery. It was believed by some of the
Eldar
that she may have been among the
Ainur
whom Melkor "had corrupted long ago"
in the
beginning
, yet she was not listed among the known Ainur.
It was later perceived by the
Valar
that she had descended from "beyond Arda"
in the "
darkness
that" lay around it when Melkor first gazed upon the
Kingdom of Manwë
in envy.
During the
Years of the Trees
, Ungoliant became the "mistress of her own lust" after disowning Melkor as her Master. In order to "feed her emptiness" she desired to take everything she could. Upon fleeing to the south, Ungoliant had coincidentally escaped "the
assaults
of the
Valar
and the hunters of
Oromë
" since their vigilance ignored the south. Later, she made her way slowly towards
Aman
, desiring the light and hating it.
Upon reaching the
Blessed Realm
, Ungoliant settled down secretly in a ravine within
Avathar
south of
Hyarmentir
, taking on the shape of "a spider of monstrous form". As she spun "her black webs in a
cleft
of the mountains", she sucked up all the light in the region, before spinning the light into "
dark nets of strangling gloom
". Eventually, no more light was able to enter her abode. As a result, she grew famished and could no longer depart from her dwelling
.
Alliance with Melkor
Eventually, after Melkor had fled from
Valinor
and came to Avathar in "the form that he had worn as the tyrant of
Utumno
", Ungoliant saw his approach and "shrank into her deepest lair" in fear, believing that Melkor planed to murder her for deserting him.
When Ungoliant refused to leave her lair after Melkor taunted and commanded her to emerge, Melkor threatened to bury her "under black stone" whilst enticing her with two shining green gems.
When Ungoliant slowly emerged,
Melkor withheld the gems
and plotted his revenge with Ungoliant.
However, upon understanding Melkor's true purpose of destroying the
Two Trees of Valinor
, a great fear overcame her as she was unwilling to risk the power of the Valar and come out of hiding. It was only when Melkor had personally vowed to use both of his hands to satisfy her lust with whatever she demanded, that Ungoliant agreed to aid him.
After devouring the two green gems amongst others that Melkor had stolen from the
Ñoldor
,
Ungoliant regained her strength
and shrouded both herself and Melkor in a cloak of
Unlight
. She spun her webs as she slowly climbed upwards until they reached the summit of Mount Hyarmentir, overlooking Valinor. She then created a ladder of woven ropes, which Melkor used to climb up the mountain to enter Valinor. It was during a
high feast
when Ungoliant and Melkor came upon the
Ezellohar
. Ungoliant cast her Unlight against the roots of the Two Trees as while Melkor came onto the mound, slaying the Two Trees with his black spear. The sap of the Two Trees poured like blood upon Ezellohar where Ungoliant sucked it up. After which, she drained each tree from their wounds, releasing "poison from her foul lips into their tissues"
causing them to slowly
wither. Yet the thirst of Ungoliant was not satisfied and she drained the
Wells of Varda
as well, belching black vapors as she drank, growing to a vast and hideous shape beyond "her most lustful dream"
that terrified even Melkor.
After this terrible act, Ungoliant prevented Melkor from forsaking her
and went with him to
Formenos
while her Unlight stymied the pursuit of Oromë and
Tulkas
.
While there, Melkor allowed her to feast on
Fëanor
's great treasury as a distraction as he stole the
Silmarils
.
Ungoliant and Melkor then fled
Aman
through
Araman
and
Oiomúrë
, eventually crossing over the
Helcaraxë
to escape justice at the hands of the Valar.
Ungoliant attacks Morgoth
Balrogs of Morgoth attack Ungoliant
Upon arriving in Middle-earth, Ungoliant perceived that Melkor was hoping to escape from her to the ruins of
Angband
where the remnants of his forces awaited him. Suspecting that he intended to leave his promise to her unfulfilled, she forced Melkor to halt in his tracks, and demanded that he fulfill his vow to satisfy her hunger with both of his hands. Inevitably, the
Dark Lord
indeed surrendered to her the many gems that the Ñoldor had at
Formenos
. However, as Ungoliant devoured them and grew even larger and darker, she noticed that Melkor was only using his left hand to feed her. When she demanded that he open his right hand, Melkor refused, claiming that he named the
objects in his right hand
onto himself, and that he no longer needed her aid.
As a consequence of the power Ungoliant had gained and Melkor had lost,
she
attacked
him, overpowering him and wrapping him in a "web of clinging throngs" in an effort to strangle him. The attempt on his own life caused Melkor to release an echoing cry of desperation through the mountains. The cry was so great and dreadful that the very land it was uttered in became known as
Lammoth
ever after. Unfortunately for Ungoliant, Melkor's cry of desperation had been heard by the
Balrogs
beneath the ruins of Angband. As tempests of fire, the Balrogs came swiftly to Lammoth to save the return of their lord. The webs of Ungoliant were smote asunder by their whips of flame, and forcing her to break off her attack. She fled in fear from them, covering herself with the black vapors which she belched. The Balrogs prepared to pursue and destroy her, but they were checked by Morgoth, who ordered them to return with him to Angband.
Final years and legacy
Upon being driven off by the Balrogs in Lammoth, Ungoliant fled south to
Beleriand
. Under a "terror of darkness", she tried to enter the
Forest of Neldoreth
in the
realm
of
Thingol
, but was thwarted by the power of the
Maia
Melian
. Yet she made a dwelling in the mountains south of
Dorthonion
, and no Elf went there, naming those mountains the
Ered Gorgoroth
.
There, Ungoliant met, mated, and eventually devoured the "other evil creatures of spider-form"
whom had dwelt there since Angband was first delved. She had many
offspring
with them, including Shelob,
before she eventually departed from that place, eventually heading to "the forgotten South of the world"
shortly before the rising of the
Sun
, where she would disappear from history.
While the fate of Ungoliant is not told in any tale,
it was believed that she eventually let her ever growing hunger overcome her and she devoured herself "in her uttermost famine".
The offspring that Ungoliant had beneath Ered Gorgoroth eventually spread throughout the dark valley, infesting it and giving to it a reputation as a place of horror, as well as the name of
Nan Dungortheb
.
At an unknown time after the
Darkening of Valinor
, a
Vanyarin
Elf
named
Elemmírë
wrote the
Aldudénië
, which told of the role of Ungoliant's Unlight in the death of the Two Trees.
Etymology
Ungoliant
is a
Sindarin
name meaning "gloom spider"
or "Gloomweaver";
it is a loan from
Ungoliantë
in
Quenya
.
It consists of the words
ungol
("spider")
and
iant
("arch;
crossing, ford;
bridge")
.
Del-du-thling
is a
Sindarin
name possibly meaning "horror night spider" and consisting of the elements
del
("horror"),
dû
("dim, dark;
dimness,
night;
night-fall, late evening"), and
thling
("spider, spider’s web, cobweb")
.
Ungoliantë
is a
Quenya
name meaning "Gloomweaver",
"Great Spider Who Enmeshes",
and possibly "shadow spider".
It consists of the words
ungwë
("spider
gloom;
spider's web")
or
ungo
("cloud, dark shadow")
and
liantë
("tendril;
vine;
spider")
.
In other versions
In the state of the
mythology
in 1916, when
J.R.R. Tolkien
had gone to serve in the
First World War
, the first precursor to Ungoliant was
Ungwë-Tuita
, the
Spider of Night
, who once was close to ensnaring
Eärendil
.
Even earlier, before the
The Book of Lost Tales
, she was called
Gungliont
. In that version, after helping Melkor destroy the Two Trees, she left, instead of attacking Melkor as in later versions. The Eldar called her
Virilomë
, or
Wirilomë
in
Qenya
.
In
Sindarin
, this name is translated into
Gwerlum
.
Analysis
According to "Ungoliant", an essay by John Wm. Houghton, the story of Ungoliant and Morgoth is comparable to the account in
John Milton
's
Paradise Lost
in which
Sin
conceives a child, Death, by
Satan
. Both Sin and Death are always hungry; Satan says he will feed them, and leads them to the world.
In a
1989
article in
Mythlore
, Joe Abbott, wrote that Ungoliant and Shelob are the "product of a singular concept" and thus similar monsters.
He observed that they are comparable to the
female giants
from Northern folklore; though such giants are not usually in spider form, he notes an early Icelandic example where "the Devil appears as a spider and has his leg cut off".
On Ungoliant's race, Abbott noted Tolkien's remark in
The Theft of Melko
from
The Book of Lost Tales
that
Mayhap she was bred of mists and darkness on the confines of the Shadowy seas, in the utter dark that came between the overthrow of the Lamps and the kindling of the Trees, but
more like she has always been
[Abbott's italics]; and she it is who loveth still to dwell in that black place
taking the guise
of an unlovely spider.
Drawing attention to Tolkien's suggestion that Ungoliant had always existed and that she simply chose to appear as a spider, Abbott stated that this must mean she was one of the Maiar.
He also suggested that Ungoliant is possibly a parallel of Nott ("Night"), an Icelandic female giant in the "Gilfaginning" in the
Prose Edda
of
Snorri Sturluson
.
Nott was dark, like all her kindred, just as Ungoliant and all her brood dwelled in and personified darkness.
In a
2007
article in
Mythlore
, Candice Fredrick and Sam McBride wrote that Ungoliant and Shelob signified purely irrational evil, "wholly preoccupied with their own lusts; they operate on the pleasure principle".
This is in contrast with Melkor and Sauron who, while also wholly evil, are "evil guided by rationality" as they possessed the power of rational thought.
Thus, Ungoliant chooses "instant gratification" while Melkor can think long-term, exploiting other beings to achieve his goals.
Fredrick and McBride further asserted that the spiders' irrationality required Tolkien to make them female, while the Dark Lords' analytical thought required Tolkien to make them male.
In a
2008
article in
Mallorn
, Kristine Larsen wrote that the mention in
Morgoth's Ring
of Varda (and Manwë) having power over Ungoliant is unique.
In adaptations
Middle-earth Role Playing
In the
Middle-earth Role Playing
, Ungoliant is considered to be a
Demon
of the
Void
and the incarnation of darkness and destruction.
She was also known as
Îavarauko
(
Q
. "Demon of the Void"),
Úcal
(
Q
. "Unlight"), the Ever-hungry, and
Úcalatári
(
Q
. "Queen of the Unlight").
Minas Morgul
The seventh track of
Summoning
's
1995
album
Minas Morgul
is called "Ungolianth", a deliberate misspelling of Ungoliant. The lyrics reference Ungoliant as well as Avathar and Morgoth after he had claimed the Silmarils.
Nightfall in Middle-Earth
Ungoliant's conflict with Morgoth over the Silmaril was the subject of
Blind Guardian
's song "Into the Storm", the second track in their
1998
album
Nightfall in Middle-Earth
.
The Morrigan's Call
On their
2006
album
The Morrigan's Call
, the Irish Celtic metal band
Cruachan
featured a song called "Ungoliant" as well as one named after Shelob.
The Hobbit: An Unexpected Journey
In
Peter Jackson
's
2012
film
,
Radagast
describes the spiders he saw as "some kind of spawn of Ungoliant" while discussing the darkness that has fallen over
Mirkwood
.
Middle-earth: Shadow of Mordor
In the
2014
video-game,
Middle-earth: Shadow of Mordor
, Ungoliant is mentioned twice: The first time is in a memory attached to the "Crushed Ungol Egg" artifact, during which
Sauron
's servant addresses Shelob as the "last daughter of Ungoliant". She is mentioned again when
Talion
and the
Wraith
discuss the Ungol spiders that infest
Mordor
.
The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power
In the
third episode
of
The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power - Season Two
,
Shelob
lived in the
Black Forest
in the mountains
of Mordor.
There, she made her nest in a
cave
with some spiderlings.
Ungoliant is a giant spider whose offspring was Shelob,
although this is never mentioned in the episode. The "claustrophobic cave" has a connection to Ungoliant's fate.
Behind the scenes
On
July 26
of
2024
, when Juliette Harrisson interviewed
Maxim Baldry
, who portrayed Isildur, and senior VFX supervisor Jason Smith on
Den of Geek
, it is mentioned at the conclusion that the "claustrophobic cave set" holds a clue to Ungoliant's fate,
although it isn't clear what this clue actually is.

Urthel
Biographical information
Birth
Late
First Age
?
Death
FA 460
Spouse
Unknown
Weapon
Bow
,
sword
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Edain
(
House of Bëor
,
XII Bëorings
)
Urthel
was an
Edain
of the
House of Bëor
and one of the
XII Bëorings
who followed
Barahir
in the
First Age
.
Biography
After the
Dagor Bragollach
, Urthel remained with
Barahir
and his company, who refused to surrender or submit to the will of
Morgoth
, and thus they became outlaws, surviving for nearly five years in the
barren highlands
above the forest of
Dorthonion
, then in the hands of
Morgoth
. He and the others were eventually betrayed to
Sauron
by the unhappy
Gorlim
and killed at
Tarn Aeluin
.
References

======================================================================
PAGE: Urwen
CATEGORIES: Characters in The Children of Húrin, Characters in Unfinished Tales, The Silmarillion characters

======================================================================

Urwen
Biographical information
Other names
Lalaeth,
Lalaith
Birth
FA 466
Death
FA 469
Realms
Dor-lómin
Parentage
Húrin
and
Morwen
Siblings
Túrin
and
Niënor
Age
3
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Female
Hair
Yellow
Culture
Edain
(
House of Hador
, Men of
Dor-lómin
)
Urwen
, also known as
Lalaeth
or
Lalaith
, was an
Edain
child of the
House of Hador
, the second child of
Húrin
and
Morwen
and the sister of
Túrin
and
Niënor
during the
First Age
.
Biography
Urwen was nicknamed
Lalaith
, because her laughter was compared to the merry sound of the
Nen Lalaith
,
a stream from
Amon Darthir
that ran by the walls of Húrin's house in
Dor-lómin
, because she was a happy child and brought much joy to the hearts of the household of Húrin during her short life. Her hair was yellow like the lilies that grew in the grass fields of
Dor-lómin
. She often took to "going upon grass or under tree", singing old songs from long ago that were made when the Edain first learned "the tongue of the
Elves
". It was because of this that Húrin once said to Morwen that Urwen was as "fair as an
Elf-child
…but briefer".
During her short life, Túrin gave "all the warmth of his heart" to Urwen, though he rarely played with her, preferring instead "to guard her unseen and" watching her sing.
Before the end of the
Year of Lamentation
, an sickness known as "the
Evil Breath
came to Dor-lómin" from
Angband
and both Urwen and Túrin fell ill. Sometime while Túrin still "lay long in a fever and dark dream", the Evil Breath took Urwen's life. Before Túrin recovered, Morwen blamed her death on the
Enemy
and forbade the household from speaking Urwen's nickname.
After Túrin was healed and "asked for Lalaith", his nurse relayed Morwen's command to him. When Túrin went and asked Morwen why he could not speak his sister's nickname, she told him that "Urwen is dead, and laughter is stilled in this house". Húrin tried to "make a song of lamentation", but his harp broke instead, causing him to curse "the
Marrer of Middle-earth
". Though Túrin abided by Morwen's command, he wept for Urwen bitterly alone at night. He later revealed to
Sador
that he had imagined that Urwen would grow up to look like one of the
Eldar
.
Túrin never got over her death and always looked for her likeness in women ever after.
During his time in
Nargothrond
, Túrin spoke of Urwen to
Finduilas
, confessing to her that she reminded him of how he imagined his sister would look grown up.
Upon finding
Níniel
in the
Forest of Brethil
,
Túrin mistook her as being similar to both Urwen and Morwen "in her looks and ways",
and thus tragically causing him to wed her a few years later.
Etymology
Urwen
was an unglossed
Sindarin
name that may mean "fire maiden", containing the elements
ûr
("fire")
and
gwen(d)
("maiden").
Lalaith
was a nickname derived from the Sindarin noun meaning "Laughter".
Lalaeth
was a similar name to
Lalaith
, though it is not told whether it was a replacement or a variation of
Lalaith
.
The Heraldic Device of Hador Lórindol
The
House of Hador
was previously known as the
House of Marach
.

PAGE: Vairë
CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth, The Silmarillion characters, Valar

======================================================================

This article is about the
the
Valier
. For the  wife of
Lindo
, see
Vairë (Elf)
.
Vairë
Biographical information
Other names
Gwîr,
The Weaver
Titles
Queen of the Valar
Birth
Before the creation of Arda
Death
Immortal
Spouse
Mandos
Physical description
Race
Ainur
Culture
Valier
Vairë
(
Quenya
;
IPA
:
[ˈvaɪre]
or
[ˈwaɪre]
- "Weaver") was an
Ainu
and
Vala
responsible for the weaving of the story of
Arda
.
Biography
Vairë was counted among the
Valier
, the
Queens
of the
Valar
, though she was not as great in power or prestige as some. Her "storied webs" covered the
Halls of Mandos
, where she apparently lived with
Mandos
, her husband.
With the passage of time and its many
Ages
, her woven tapestries expanded and clothed all the walls of the Halls of the Dead.
After the death of
Finwë
,
Míriel
returned to life and dwelt in the house of Vairë. She was given the task to record the deeds of the
House of Finwë
.
Etymology
In
Quenya
,
Vairë
means "Weaver", and was derived from the Primitive Quendian
weirē
, which derives from the root
wey
("weave, wind"). Her Ñoldorin name is
Gwîr
, from
gwi
("net, web").

PAGE: Vairë (Elf)
CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth, Elves

======================================================================

This article is about the
wife of the
Elf
Lindo
. For the the
Valier
, see
Vairë
.
!
This content is considered pre-canon.
While the subject of this article is based on official information, it was replaced, emended, or abandoned in later stages of the
legendarium
.
!
Vairë
Biographical information
Birth
YT
?
Death
Unknown
Spouse
Lindo
Physical description
Race
Elves
Gender
Female
Culture
Falmari
Elves of
Tol Eressëa
Vairë
was an
Elf
of
Tol Eressëa
and one of the keepers of the
Cottage of Lost Play
.
Biography
Vairë was the daughter of
Tulkastor
, one of the earliest dwellers of Tol Eressëa. She married
Lindo
and lived in the Cottage with her husband where they both entertained many visitors, including the hosting of the
Man
Ælfwine
(Eriol) who visited there during his travels. She was very wise in many things, and talked with Ælfwine, explaining to him things about the household.
Etymology
Vairë
was a
Quenya
word that meant 'weaver'.

PAGE: Valandil
CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth, The Silmarillion characters
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Valandil
======================================================================

This article refers to the
High King of
Arnor
. For other namesakes, see
Valandil (disambiguation)
.
Valandil
Biographical information
Titles
High King of Arnor
Birth
SA 3430
Rule
TA 10
-
TA 249
(239 Years)
Death
TA 249
Realms
Arnor
Spouse
Unnamed wife
Age
260
Weapon
King's
sword
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Dúnedain of the North
(
House of Isildur
)
Valandil
was the third
High King of Arnor
.
Valandil was the youngest of
Isildur
's four sons, he was only a child at the time of the
War of the Last Alliance
, in which his grandfather,
Elendil
, was killed. Due to this, he remained in
Rivendell
when his father and brothers went into the south, and was thus the only one of Isildur's sons to survive the
Disaster of the Gladden Fields
. He received the
Shards of Narsil
as an heirloom of the
Heirs of Isildur
when he came of age.
Because the
Elendilmir
had been lost with his father, the Elven smiths of Rivendell created a copy for Valandil, which came to be known itself as the Elendilmir.
Although Valandil was by right High King of
Arnor
and
Gondor
, Valandil never made an attempt to assert suzerainty over his cousin
Meneldil
in Gondor and thus the two realms were sundered. Valandil continued to use the title High King of Arnor, possibly to assert his rights as head of the House of Elendil.
Valandil took up the
Sceptre of Annúminas
in
TA 10
and ruled Arnor for two-hundred and thirty-nine years, which was longer than any King in the north or south, and he lived for two-hundred and sixty years dying in
TA 249
.
He was succeeded by his son
Eldacar
.
Etymology
Valandil
was a
Quenya
word that meant 'Valar-lover' or 'Friend of the Valar'.

PAGE: Valandil of Andúnië

This article refers to the
Lord of
Andúnië
. For other namesakes, see
Valandil (disambiguation)
.
Valandil
Biographical information
Titles
Lord of Andúnië
Birth
SA 630
Spouse
Unnamed wife
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Númenóreans
(
House of Elros
)
Valandil
founded the line of the
Lords of Andúnië
in the western region of
Andustar
on
Númenor
.
Biography
Valandil's father was
Elatan
of
Andúnië
, and his mother was
Silmariën
, eldest child of
Tar-Elendil
of
Númenor
. The laws of Númenor at that time would not allow Silmariën to rule as queen, so Valandil and subsequent Lords of Andúnië were not in line for royal succession.
By the end of the
Second Age
, however, Valandil's descendants would become kings in
Middle-earth
, beginning with
Elendil
, the last Lord of Andúnië and first High King of
Arnor
and
Gondor
.
Valandil
was also the name of the youngest son of
Isildur
and the grandson of Elendil, who eventually became
High King of Arnor
.

Valandil
means "Devoted to the
Valar
", from
Vala
and
-ndil
("friend, devoted to"), in
Quenya
.

Valandur
Biographical information
Titles
High King of Arnor
Birth
TA 462
Rule
TA 602
-
TA 652
(50 years)
Death
TA 652
Realms
Arnor
Spouse
Unnamed wife
Age
190
Weapon
King's
sword
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Dúnedain of the North
(
House of Isildur
)
Valandur
was the eighth
High King of Arnor
.
Biography
Valandur was born in
TA 462
to
Tarondor
and his unnamed wife.
He ascended the throne of
Arnor
in
TA 602
on the death of his father Tarondor. He was killed in a battle or a violent encounter of some sort in
TA 652
, but the exact circumstances of his death were unknown. He ruled for fifty years and lived one-hundred and ninety years. He was succeeded by his son,
Elendur
.
Etymology
The name
Valandur'
probably meant 'Servant of the Valar' in
Quenya
.

While the subject of this article is based on official information, it was replaced, emended, or abandoned in later stages of the
legendarium
.
!
Valwë
Biographical information
Other names
Gwalweg
Physical description
Race
Elves
Gender
Male
Culture
Ñoldor
Elves of
Tol Eressëa
Valwë
, also called
Gwalweg
,
was a
Ñoldorin
Elf
of
Tol Eressëa
in early stages of
J.R.R. Tolkien
's
mythology
.
Biography
Valwë fled to the
Great Lands
along with the other
Gnomes
of
Tol Eressëa
in order to help
Salmar
free the Elves enslaved by
Melko
after the
Fall of Gondolin
. The Elves were utterly defeated in the
Battle of Tasarinan
,
but Valwë managed to survive and return to Tol Eressëa alongside
Tulkastor
and
Ingil
, where he would eventually be the father of
Lindo
.
Etymology
Valwë
is a name in
Qenya
possibly meaning "the happy (or fortunate) one".
Valwë
translated to
Gwalweg
in
Gnomish
.
In other versions
Christopher Tolkien
mentioned in his commentary that though Valwë was originally written as
Manwë
, the name may have been a mistake.
References

Varda Elentári
Biographical information
Other names
Elbereth, Star-Kindler, Lady of the Stars, Tintallë,
Gilthoniel, Elentári, Fanuilos, Snow-white, The Kindler
Titles
Queen of
Arda
,
Queen of the Stars,
Queen of the Valar
Birth
Before the creation of Eä
Death
Immortal
Realms
Arda
Spouse
Manwë
Weapon
Powers of the Valar
Physical description
Race
Ainur
Culture
Valar
(
Aratar
) (Leader, Queen)
Varda
(
Quenya
: "Sublime" or "Lofty"), one of the mightiest of the
Valier
and Queen of the
Valar
, was an
Ainu
and
Aratar
, responsible for situating the stars in the heavens above
Arda
. She was also known as
Elbereth
(
Sindarin
: "Queen of the Stars"),
Tintallë
("The Kindler"),
or
Gilthoniel
, and was the spouse of
Manwë
, with whom she lived in
Ilmarin
on the summit of
Taniquetil
in
Aman
.
Biography
Varda was the most beautiful of all the Valar, and her beauty was said to be beyond the description of Men and Elves, for her face radiated the light of
Eru Ilúvatar
. Unsurprisingly, the province over which she ruled was light, to the extent where it was said that
"in light is her power and her joy".
Even before
Melkor
first began to create his discord, Varda knew his mind well, and rejected him because of it, and was always his enemy from that moment on. As a result of this, Melkor also hated Varda the most out of the Valar and feared her more than all others whom Eru had created.
Varda came to Arda with her husband, Manwë, and the other Valar in the beginning of days. She aided Manwë in the rule of Arda and the watching of
Middle-earth
. She made the
stars
, filled the
Lamps of the Valar
with light, collected the dew of the
Two Trees
in her
Wells
, fashioned the newer stars and constellations in preparation for the
Awakening of the Elves
, hallowed the
Silmarils
, established the courses of the
Moon
and
Sun
, and set
Gil-Orestel
, the Star of High Hope (later known as the Star of
Eärendil
), in the sky.
Because of her creation of the stars, the Elves of Middle-earth revere and love her the most out of the Valar. They even gave her the name of "Elbereth", invoke her in times where they require aid, and sing her praises whenever the stars rise. It was also said that when Manwë sits beside her upon their throne on Taniquetil, she was able to hear more clearly than any other ears, the prayers and laments of those in need from the furthest east even unto the west. At times, she would answer the prayers of not only
Elves
, but also those of other races. For example, during the
War of the Ring
, she aided Sam through the
Phial of Galadriel
(which contained the light of Gil-Orestel) during his struggle against
Shelob
.
Powers and Abilities
As her title of Queen of the Stars indicates, Varda has power over the stars and starlight, for she was responsible for the creation of the sun, the moon, and the stars that illuminate the sky. Her power and light were so strong and pure that even Melkor feared and hated her for her immense cosmic power. She also possessed a degree of foresight, as she was able to sense the darkness in Melkor's heart immediately, long before Middle-earth was created, which is why she distanced himself from him.
Maiar of Varda
Ilmarë
, the handmaiden of Varda and Queen of the
Maiar
alongside Eonwë, Manwë's herald.
Olórin
(Gandalf), the second-in-command (and later leader) of the
Order of Wizards
sent to Middle-earth to combat Sauron.
Etymology
Varda
means
sublime
or
lofty
in
Quenya
.
Elentári
means
queen of the stars
in Quenya.
Elbereth
means
queen of the stars
in
Sindarin
.
Gilthoniel
means
kindler of the stars
in Sindarin.
In
The Lord of the Rings
her Sindarin name,
Elbereth Gilthoniel
, appears many times, usually in a song or invocation. In
The Fellowship of the Ring
, the
Elves
whom
Frodo
,
Pippin
, and
Sam
meet in the
Shire
are singing a song in her praise. Frodo calls out to Elbereth when he is attacked at
Weathertop
, and
Aragorn
refers to her name as "deadly" to the
Nazgûl
. Frodo calls out to her when first using the
Phial of Galadriel
, in the caves of
Shelob
. Later in
The Return of the King
,  Sam suggests "Elbereth" as a password to Frodo after he frees Frodo from the Orcs in the
Tower of Cirith Ungol
; for when Frodo must let down the ladder once returning from gathering gear for two to wear in
Mordor
.
Perhaps, its Sindarin name could be related to
Ilbereth
, the name of the secretary of
Father Christmas
in
Letters from Father Christmas
.
In other versions
In earlier versions of the legendarium Manwë and Varda were called with the title of
Wanwavoisi
and they were conceived as the parents of
Eönwë
(called
Fionwë Úrion
) and Ilmarë (called
Erinti
). Tolkien later abandoned this idea of the Children of the Valar.
Inspiration
Literary connections
The Valar are divine beings below a greater, more ultimate Creator: Eru Ilúvatar. Hence, some equate the Valar of Middle-earth with saints and angels, and Varda (as the most beloved and most prayed-to Valar) may therefore be an equivalent of the Virgin Mary in Tolkien's own Catholic faith.
With his creation and inclusion of the Valar into his words, Tolkien also paid homage to Greek mythology, particularly the Greek Pantheon: each and every one of the Valar had a specific realm within the world that was at his or her command, much as the Greek Gods in ancient myths. Tolkien wanted to create a mythology for his world, and the Greek model fit the bill perfectly.
With this in mind, Manwë, King of the Valar, is most akin to Zeus (the King of the Greek Gods). In the same manner, Varda, as the wife of Manwë as well as the Queen of the Valar, is the equivalent of Hera (the wife of Zeus and Queen of the Greek Gods). Due to her being the most beautiful and lovable of the Valar, she was also seen to be most akin to Aphrodite (the Greek Goddess of Love and Beauty).

Vardamir Nólimon
Biographical information
Titles
King of Númenor
Birth
SA 61
Rule
SA 442
Death
SA 471
Spouse
Unnamed wife
Children
Tar-Amandil
,
Vardilmë
,
Aulendil
,
Nolondil
Parentage
Elros
(father)
Siblings
Tindómiel
,
Manwendil
,
Atanalcar
Age
410
Weapon
Aranrúth
Physical description
Race
Half-elves
(potentially)
Mortal fate
Culture
Númenóreans
(
House of Elros
)
Vardamir Nólimon
was the eldest child of
Elros
Tar-Minyatur, and second
King of Númenor
.
Elrond Half-elven
was his uncle. Vardamir may have been a
Half-elf
, with slow maturity like the
Elves
and ability to choose a mortal or immortal fate.
Biography
Vardamir was heir to the Kingship of
Númenor
, noted as an exceptional scholar whose only desire was to study, not to rule. When Elros died, Vardamir immediately abdicated in favour of his son,
Tar-Amandil
. He was counted as the second King of Númenor, having nominally ruled for a single year. He died in year 471 of the
Second Age
, aged 410 years.
If Vardamir was a Half-elf, he therefore chose a mortal fate.
He had three younger siblings:
Tindómiel
,
Manwendil
, and
Atanalcar
.
He had four children:
Tar-Amandil
, eldest son who would succeed his father
Vardilmë
, daughter
Aulendil
, son
Nolondil
, son
Etymology
Vardamir
is a
Quenya
name meaning "Jewel of Varda", from
Varda
, a
Valar
, and
mírë
, meaning 'jewel'.
Other versions of the legendarium
Tolkien considered giving Vardamir the choice of the Half-elven, such that the son of Elros would match the children of Elrond.
Whether he committed this idea or not is unclear, as Vardamir's three younger siblings are not mentioned in the essay explaining this.

Vardilmë
Biographical information
Birth
SA 203
Death
Early
Second Age
?
Spouse
Unknown
Physical description
Race
Possibly
Half-elves
(
mortal
fate)
Gender
Female
Culture
Númenóreans
(
House of Elros
)
Vardilmë
was the second child and only daughter of the short reigning
King
Vardamir Nólimon
.
Biography
Vardilmë was born in
Númenor
in an unknown location. Her father Vardamir was potentially
Half-elven
like her grandfather
Elros
, the first
King of Númenor
. This means she may have been a Half-elf of the third generation, giving her a slow maturity and long youth like the
Elves
, while being fated for mortality like
Men
. She had three siblings, the younger
Aulendil
,
Nolondil
, and the older
Amandil
who would later be crowned Tar-Amandil, the third King of Númenor. Nothing else is known of her life including whether she married and had children.
Etymology
The name
Vardilmë
means "Devoted to
Varda
" from the
Quenya
suffix
-dil
("friend, devoted to").

!
This content is considered pre-canon.
While the subject of this article is based on official information, it was replaced, emended, or abandoned in later stages of the
legendarium
.
!
Velindo
Biographical information
Other names
Gwilion
,
Wéland
,
Welund
Realms
Great Lands
Physical description
Race
Fays
Gender
Male
Culture
Aulë
's folk
Velindo
was a
fay
of
Aulë
's folk according to the early version of the
legendarium
associated with
The Book of Lost Tales
.
History
Velindo was a servant of the
Vala
Aulë
, who once sent him to the
Great Lands
in order to fetch "some of the good, heavy red gold of the
dwarves
". However, he grew prideful there due to the adoration of the Dwarves and
Men
owing to his skills as a craftsman. Therefore, he never returned to Aulë, but rather set up on his own - and he grew famous, but as the
Elves
faded so did his own power and fame, yet he never became wicked.
Ages later, the mariner
Eriol
asked
Rúmil
, an Elf of
Tol Eressëa
, if the person of
Wéland
, a renowned smith in the Germanic legends, was the same as Aulë. Rúmil instead corrected him, saying rather that
Wéland
was actually Velindo.
Etymology
Velindo
has an untold meaning in
Qenya
. Its equivalent in
Gnomish
is
Gwilion
.
Velindo's name in
Old English
was
Wéland
or
Welund
.
In other versions
In another contemporary text, the Germanic smith Wéland is instead identified with the
Gnome
Fëanor
. That text depicts an outline of a story placed during the wars of the Elves with
Melko
, in which Fëanor was captured by a man called
Niðad
, King of the
Niaroth
. However, Fëanor killed Niðad's sons and managed to escape by crafting artificial wings for himself; he also made love to the king's daughter
Beaduhilde
and fled with her, but ultimately lost her in woods. Eventually Beaduhilde, who was now pregnant, forgave Fëanor, and in spite of her father's wrath at her pregnancy, she still managed to obtain Niðad's blessing upon her son.
External links
"
Skilled Smiths and Princes of Elves: The Wayland-legend and the First Age of Middle-earth
" by Lillian Hammen in the second issue of Volume 9 of the Journel of Tolkien Research
References

Vidumavi
Biographical information
Other names
Galadwen
Titles
Princess of
Rhovanion
Birth
Before
TA 1250
Death
TA 1332
or
TA 1344
Spouse
Valacar
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Female
Culture
Northmen
,
Men of Gondor
Vidumavi
was a princess of the
Northmen
, the daughter of
Vidugavia
, who claimed the title of
King of Rhovanion
.
Biography
Vidumavi married the heir to
Gondor
's throne, Prince
Valacar
, when he was on a diplomatic mission to
Rhovanion
after the year
TA 1250
. The
Dúnedain
of Gondor feared that a shorter-lived bloodline would weaken Gondor's royal line.
In
TA 1255
, she bore Valacar a son and heir, the future
King
Eldacar
. Because of Eldacar's short-lived Northman mother, many of the Dúnedain and
Men of Gondor
were unwilling to accept him as king. This led to the disastrous
Kin-strife
eighty-eight years later in
TA 1432
.
She lived long for one of the
Middle Men
, although her exact date of death is unclear.
Vidumavi was also known by the name
Galadwen
after coming to Gondor with her husband and child.
Her character was described as noble and high of courage and she quickly adapted to Gondor, quickly learning its language and customs.
Etymology
The name
Vidumavi
is a Latinised version of the
Gothic
word
Widumawi
(wood-maiden).

Vorondil
Biographical information
Other names
The Hunter
Titles

Spouse
Unnamed wife
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender

Culture
Men of Gondor
(
House of Húrin
)
Vorondil
, also known as
Vorondil the Hunter
, was the
Steward to the King
Eärnil II
of
Gondor
.
Biography
Born in
TA 1919
, Vorondil was the son of the Steward
Pelendur
.
Following the death of his father, Pelendur in
TA 1998
Vorondil succeeded him and became the Steward to King Eärnil II. Vorondil served as the Steward to King Eärnil II until his death in
TA 2029
at the age of 110 years old.
Vorondil was succeeded by his son
Mardil Voronwë
who later became the first of the Ruling Stewards.
Vorondil was called the hunter because of his prowess hunting. He hunted the
Kine of Araw
, and from its horn was fashioned the
Great Horn
.

PAGE: Voronwë
CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth, Characters in Unfinished Tales, Elves, Elves of Beleriand, Elves of Gondolin, The Silmarillion characters
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Voronwë
======================================================================

This article is about the
Ñoldo
. For the other namesakes, see
Voronwë (disambiguation)
.
Voronwë
Biographical information
Other names
Voronwë Aranwion
Birth
First Age
?
Death
Unknown
Physical description
Race
Elves
Gender
Male
Eyes
Sea-grey
Culture
Ñoldor
(
House of Fingolfin
)
Gondolindrim
Voronwë
, also called
Aranwion
, was a
Ñoldorin
Elf
mariner from
Gondolin
who was one of a crew of several
ships
sent by
Turgon
to solicit aid from the
Valar
. The
Doom of Mandos
, however, made any such attempt futile, and Voronwë was the only one to survive.
Biography
Voronwë sitting by the sea after the foundering of his ship, by Filat
Voronwë was relatively young according to the measure of the Elves, having been born in
Middle-earth
(and specifically in
Nevrast
) instead of
Valinor
. His father was
Aranwë
, a Ñoldo, but his mother was of the
Sindarin
Elves of the Falas
, and kinswoman of
Círdan
. He called himself "of the
House of Fingolfin
", which in this case means a follower of that house, rather than a relationship of blood.
Voronwë was saved by
Ulmo
with the express purpose of leading
Tuor
to
Gondolin
, which he did.
During the
Fall of Gondolin
, Voronwë was asked by Tuor to guard
Idril
. He escaped the sack, following Tuor and Idril through the
secret way
. He led them until
Sirion
and they did not continue, for Voronwë did not know the regions beyond the river.
It was said that Voronwë sailed west with
Eärendil
in
Vingilot
.
Etymology
His name is
Quenya
in origin.
Voronwë
means "the Steadfast", "the Faithful", or "the Constant",
and his surname
Aranwion
means "son of Aranwë". The name
Voronwë
also appears as an
epessë
(honourary title) of various other characters; for example,
Mardil Voronwë
.
He called himself
Bronweg
, the
Sindarin
version of his name.
It also means "one of steadfastness".
Other versions
In writings given in
The Book of Lost Tales
, Bronweg was the father of
Ilfrin
, gong-warden of
Tol Eressëa
.

PAGE: Vána
CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth, The Silmarillion characters, Valar
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Vána
======================================================================

Vána
Biographical information
Other names
The Ever-young
the Fair
Queen of Flowers
Titles
A Queen of the Valar
Birth
Before the creation of
Arda
Death
Immortal
Realms
Valinor
Spouse
Oromë
Physical description
Race
Ainur
Hair
Golden
Culture
Valier
Vána
(
Quenya
;
IPA
:
[ˈvaːna]
- "Beautiful One") was an
Ainu
and a
Valië
who was responsible for the preserving of the youth made for all life in
Arda
. Among the seven
Valier
, Vána was considered the sixth in importance.
She was the sister of
Yavanna
and wife of
Oromë
and was possibly related to
Melian
.
Biography
Vána was the younger sister of Yavanna (
Queen of the Earth
) and the wife of Oromë (
Huntsman of the Valar
). In her dwellings she had gardens filled with golden flowers, and she frequently came to the forests of Oromë. Like her sister, the province of Vána was growing things of the world, and she was a lover of nature. Vána had influence with the flora and fauna of Middle-earth, and she was associated especially with flowers; thus Vána was also known as the "Queen of Flowers".
It was said that when the Valar
Tulkas
and
Nessa
were wedded on the Isle of
Almaren
, the first dwelling of the Valar, Vána robed her sister-in-law with her flowers.
In the days of the
Two Trees of Valinor
,
Arien
of the
Maiar
had served Vána by tending to the golden flowers of her gardens, watering them with bright dews from
Laurelin
.
Melian
initially served Vána before departing for
Middle-earth
.
After the
Darkening of Valinor
and the flight of the
Ñoldor
to Middle-earth, most of the Valar were glad to have their ancient peace back, wishing for neither
Melkor
and his violence nor the murmur of the restless Ñoldor to come upon them again. Therefore, they clamored for the concealment of their land
Aman
. It was said that Vána was one among them.
Character
According to
The Silmarillion
, "All flowers spring as Vána passes and opens if she glances upon them; and all birds sing at her coming."
Vána robed herself in flowers and it was said that her hair was golden in color.
Vána had the beauty of both heaven and earth upon her face and in all her works.
Like Nessa, Vána also sang and danced along with her maidens.
Maiar of Vána
Arien
, the Maia who guided the
Sun
, was one of her Maiar in
Valinor
.
Melian
, who later in Middle-earth became Queen of
Doriath
by marrying the Elf-King
Thingol
.
Etymology
The name
Vána
may have been derived from the
Quenya
word
vanima
("beautiful"), as she is often described as "fair".
Hence she was also known as
Vána the Fair
.
Other versions of the legendarium
In an early version of the
legendarium
, Vána was a character of much greater power, known as
Tári-Laisi
, queen "of life" and bringer of spring and happiness. She was of a pair of sisters simply known as Tári ("Queens"), with the other being an early version of Nienna, seen as a mistress of death, winter, and despair.
Vána and her husband
Oromë
were once envisioned to have had a daughter named
Nielíqui
.
In other material, Vána was the younger sister of Queen
Varda
and
Yavanna
.
Vána had played a formative role in the growth of great golden Tree
Laurelin
: 
"Then was the pit covered with rich earths that
Palúrien
(Yavanna) devised, and Vána came who loveth life and sunlight and at whose song the flowers arise and open, and the murmur of her maidens round her was like to the merry noise of the folk that stir abroad for the first time on a bright morning. There sang she the song of spring upon the mound, and danced about it, and watered it with great streams of that golden light that
Ulmo
(
Lord of All Waters
) had brought from the spilled lakes--as yet was
Kulullin
almost o'erflowing at that time.
In earlier versions laid out in
The History of Middle-earth
,
J.R.R. Tolkien
wrote that when the great
Two Trees of Valinor
were destroyed, Vána fell into inconsolable grief for her great love for the golden tree Laurelin. Furthermore, Vána then attempted to gather what she could of the spilled light in order to rekindle the dead tree. Yet, as Vána clung to the dead trunk of Laurelin after all ministrations had failed to rekindle the tree, her tears finally coaxed forth a new golden shoot that bore one golden fruit. The
Valar
used this golden fruit to construct the
Sun
that Vána's maiden
Urwen
(
Arien
) would later lead across the sky. Then Vána, in repentance of her earlier doubts, cut short her golden
hair to weave the sails of the Sun-ship.
Gallery
Vána, by losse-elda
Vána the Ever-young, by Emberrose Art

======================================================================
PAGE: Vëannë
CATEGORIES: Characters in Beren and Lúthien, Characters in The Book of Lost Tales, Elves
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Vëannë
======================================================================

!
This content is considered pre-canon.
While the subject of this article is based on official information, it was replaced, emended, or abandoned in later stages of the
legendarium
.
!
Vëannë
(named
Melinir
by
Eriol
) was a child who resided at the
Cottage of Lost Play
in
Tol Eressëa
.
The Tale of Tinúviel, the earliest version of the story of
Beren
and
Lúthien
, was once narrated by Vëannë at the cottage, on the same night that Littleheart afterwards told the tale of the
Fall of Gondolin
.
Canon
She appears only in
J.R.R. Tolkien
's early stories of
Eriol
in
The Book of Lost Tales
, composed between 1910 and 1920. She also is reintroduced into the 'complete tale' of
Beren and Lúthien
, again as the teller of The Tale of Tinúviel.
References

PAGE: Vëantur
CATEGORIES: Characters in Unfinished Tales, Númenóreans, The Silmarillion characters

Vëantur
Biographical information
Titles
Captain of the King's Ships
Birth
SA 451
Death
Early
Second Age
?
Spouse
Unnamed wife
Weapon
Unknown
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Númenóreans
Vëantur
was the
Númenórean
captain of the royal fleet of
Tar-Elendil
. He was likewise grandfather of
Tar-Aldarion
. Vëantur was also the commander of the first ship that returned to
Middle-earth
.
Biography
Vëantur was a great Mariner and Navigator, serving as
Captain of the King's Ships
under Tar-Elendil, the fourth King of
Númenor
, and the first of the Númenoreans to achieve a voyage back to Middle-earth, in the year
SA 600
of the
Second Age
. His
ships
' names were
Entulessë
and
Númerrámar
, which were docked at a quay attached to his house, located in the southern side of the firth of
Rómenna
. He had a daughter named
Almarian
who married
Tar-Meneldur
, relating him to the royal family.
On his subsequent travels, he took his grandson Anardil with him on a journey to
Lindon
in
SA 725
. Anardil would later become Tar-Aldarion and found the
Guild of Venturers
.

Walda
Biographical information
Titles
King of Rohan
Birth
TA 2780
Rule
TA 2842
Death
TA 2851
Spouse
Unnamed wife
Weapon
King's
sword
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Rohirrim
(
House of Eorl
)
Walda
was the twelfth
King of Rohan
.
Biography
Walda was born in
TA 2780
and was the son of
Brytta Léofa
. As Prince of
Rohan
, he served in the army and helped to defend the realm from the
Orcs
that tried to invade Rohan during King Brytta's reign. After his father's death in
TA 2842
, Walda succeeded to the throne at the age of sixty-two.
Some Orcs remained in Rohan, however; and nine years later after his succession in
TA 2851
, Walda was killed during an Orc attack in
Dunharrow
. His son
Folca
then became King and avenged his death.
With a reign of only nine years, King Walda has the shortest reign of all the recorded Kings of Rohan.
Other versions of the legendarium
In Tolkien's earlier works, the eleventh King of Rohan, Walda's father, was initially named
Brego
, then
Hama
.

PAGE: Yavanna
CATEGORIES: Characters in The History of Middle-earth, The Silmarillion characters, Valar
